did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models, specifically Vision Transformers (ViT) and ResNet50, integrated with ABCD clinical features in an ensemble architecture (HEVA) for skin cancer diagnosis. It also employs a SegFormer-B5 model for semantic segmentation of skin lesions. The study evaluates these AI methods on dermatological datasets (HAM-10000 and ISIC 2018) to classify skin lesions and melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It proposes a new architecture (HEVA), describes specific methods (integration of ViT, ResNet50, and ABCD features), details the experimental setup (datasets HAM-10000 and ISIC 2018, training workflow, classifiers), and reports original quantitative results (e.g., 93.2% accuracy on HAM-10000). While it includes a 'Related Work' section, its primary purpose is to report new technical contributions and validation, not to summarize prior literature.

title: HEVA: Integrating ViT and ResNet50 with ABCD Clinical Features for Skin Cancer Diagnosis
journal: Revista de Informática Teórica e Aplicada - RITA
brief_summary: This study proposes HEVA, a hybrid ensemble vision architecture that integrates Vision Transformers (ViT) and ResNet50 with handcrafted ABCD clinical descriptors for skin cancer diagnosis. The method utilizes a SegFormer-B5 model to generate high-quality lesion masks for preprocessing and feature extraction. The approach was evaluated on the HAM-10000 dataset and tested for cross-dataset generalization on the ISIC 2018 dataset. On HAM-10000, the HEVA architecture achieved 93.2% accuracy and an 84.2% F1-score using an SVM classifier with a sigmoid kernel. For the ISIC 2018 test set, the model attained 87.7% accuracy and an 82.01% macro F1-score, demonstrating robust performance across different datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: Grouped into the 'Malignant' category for binary classification. Identified as the deadliest skin cancer and primary focus of early detection efforts.
Grouped into the 'Benign' category. Distinguished from melanoma in the introduction as a key diagnostic challenge.
Grouped into the 'Malignant' category. Listed as one of the types of skin cancer in the introduction.
Grouped into the 'Benign' category in the HAM-10000 dataset distribution.
Grouped into the 'Benign' category in the HAM-10000 dataset distribution.
Grouped into the 'Malignant' category in the HAM-10000 dataset distribution.
Grouped into the 'Malignant' category in this study's conservative approach, despite generally being considered harmless, to prioritize sensitivity.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'Benign' and 'Malignant' categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes the HEVA architecture for the classification of skin lesions. The HAM-10000 dataset classes were binned into two categories: Benign and Malignant. The model uses an SVM classifier with a sigmoid kernel to predict these discrete diagnostic labels. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

all_task_types.target: Binary semantic segmentation of skin lesions (lesion vs. background).
all_task_types.details: A SegFormer-B5 model was trained to generate high-quality lesion masks for preprocessing and feature extraction. This is a secondary task to support the primary classification pipeline. Metrics reported include mean IoU (82.6%) and mean accuracy (91.2%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 10,015 dermatoscopic images collected from diverse populations and acquired by different modalities. It contains seven original classes of skin lesions, which were binned into two categories (Benign and Malignant) for this study.

use.training: Used for training the HEVA architecture components (ViT, ResNet50) and the SegFormer-B5 segmentation model. A subset of 9,947 images with adequate segmentation masks was selected for training. Data augmentation strategies (Original, Augmented Equalized, Augmented Original) were applied.
use.validation: Used for validation via 3-Fold cross-validation to identify the best-performing subset and select the final model based on F1-Score.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the HEVA architecture, achieving 93.2% accuracy and 84.2% F1-score.
use.other: Used to train the SegFormer-B5 model for generating lesion masks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public external dataset used for cross-dataset evaluation to test the generalization of the HEVA architecture. Ground-truth masks were not directly available for all images, so predicted masks from the trained SegFormer-B5 model were used.

use.testing: Used as an external test set to evaluate cross-dataset generalization. The model achieved 87.7% accuracy and 82.01% macro F1-score on this set.
use.other: Used to validate the robustness of the preprocessing pipeline (specifically the SegFormer-B5 generated masks) on unseen data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To identify the best-performing subset of data and select the final model based on the F1-Score metric, reducing bias from train-validation split variability.
To standardize handcrafted features to a distribution with zero mean and unit standard deviation, ensuring compatibility with machine learning models.
To increase the diversity of training examples and reduce bias in favor of the majority class when combined with oversampling.
To create a balanced training set with a 1:1 ratio between benign and malignant classes in the 'Augmented Equalized' strategy.
To guarantee the reproducibility of experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using standard metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score) and comparative analysis against baseline models and literature, but no statistical significance testing was employed.

HAM-10000.description: Binary classification of skin lesions into Benign and Malignant categories. Evaluated using 3-Fold cross-validation. Final model selected based on F1-Score.

heva_architecture.description: Hybrid Ensemble Vision Architecture integrating ViT, ResNet50, and ABCD clinical features. Best performance achieved with SVM classifier using Sigmoid kernel and Augmented Original data distribution.

HAM-10000.notes: The HEVA architecture with SVM Sigmoid kernel and Augmented Original distribution achieved the highest performance. The paper notes that ViT with Augmented Original distribution showed the highest F1-Score (82.38%) and Accuracy (92.56%) among base models.

ISIC_2018.description: Cross-dataset evaluation on 1,512 images. Lesion masks generated by SegFormer-B5. Classes mapped to Benign and Malignant (Vascular Lesions considered Malignant).
ISIC_2018.notes: The model demonstrated robust performance under cross-dataset conditions despite different class mapping compared to literature.

Segmentation_Task.description: Semantic segmentation of skin lesions using SegFormer-B5 model. Evaluated on held-out test set.
Segmentation_Task.notes: SegFormer-B5 was trained on HAM-10000 masks and used to generate masks for ISIC 2018 test set.

architecture_used.pretraining: ViT: 'vit-base-patch16-224-in21k' pre-trained on ImageNet 21k (via HuggingFace). ResNet50: Pre-trained on ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: 100x100 pixels (after preprocessing/cropping), resized to 224x224 pixels for ViT input.

components: Vision Transformer (ViT): Extracts global relationships via CLS token, resulting in a 768-dimensional representation.
ResNet50: Extracts local patterns via convolutional layers, followed by Global Average Pooling and a dense layer of 512 neurons with ReLU activation.

Handcrafted Descriptors (ABCD): 115-dimensional vector including LBP (10-bin histogram), GLCM (20 features), Wavelet statistics, HOG, HSV color stats, Morphological (circularity, asymmetry), and Fractal Dimension.
architecture_details.segmentation_model: SegFormer-B5 (pre-trained) used for generating lesion masks.

ViT.optimizer: Adam (implied by context of DL training, though not explicitly named for ViT, standard for PyTorch/HF)

classification_or_output_head.type: Ensemble Classifier applied to concatenated features
other_evaluated_classifiers: MLP (3 dense layers: 1024, 512, 1; ReLU/Sigmoid; Dropout 0.3/0.2; Adam; Binary Cross-Entropy; 10 epochs; batch 32)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SVM (Support Vector Machine) with Sigmoid kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Lesion mask generation using SegFormer-B5 semantic segmentation model
Bounding box computation from segmentation masks
Cropping to tight bounding box to remove background artifacts (rulers, color charts, vignetting, hair)
Resizing to 100x100 pixels using nearest-neighbor interpolation
Application of Gaussian filter to reduce noise while preserving edges
Resizing to 224x224 pixels for ViT input
Normalization to range [-1, 1] for ViT input
StandardScaler normalization for handcrafted features (zero mean, unit variance)
Oversampling to balance class distribution (1:1 ratio)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, zoom, translation, and mirroring were applied to increase training example diversity.
Used to create a balanced training set with a 1:1 ratio between benign and malignant classes in the 'Augmented Equalized' strategy.

Three distribution strategies were used: 'Original' (no augmentation), 'Augmented Equalized' (oversampling to 1:1 class balance), and 'Augmented Original' (geometric transforms applied to original distribution).

conclusions: The proposed HEVA architecture, which integrates Vision Transformers (ViT) and ResNet50 with handcrafted ABCD clinical descriptors, achieved high performance in skin lesion classification, reaching 93.2% accuracy and 84.2% F1-score on the HAM-10000 dataset.

The model demonstrated robust cross-dataset generalization, attaining 87.7% accuracy and 82.01% macro F1-score on the external ISIC 2018 test set, despite differences in class mapping (e.g., treating vascular lesions as malignant).

The integration of deep learning models with interpretable dermatological features (ABCD) enhances the robustness, precision, and interpretability of the classification system compared to using deep learning models alone.

The use of a SegFormer-B5 model for semantic segmentation successfully generated high-quality lesion masks (82.6% mean IoU) that generalized across datasets, enabling effective preprocessing and feature extraction for unseen data.

Among the evaluated classifiers, the Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Sigmoid kernel trained on the 'Augmented Original' data distribution provided the best performance, balancing precision and recall effectively for medical diagnosis.

Data augmentation strategies that preserve the original class distribution ('Augmented Original') were found to be more effective for generalization than those that alter class proportions ('Augmented Equalized'), as they maintain natural statistical relationships while enriching training diversity.
important_remarks: The study adopts a conservative clinical classification strategy by grouping 'Vascular Lesions' (vasc) into the 'Malignant' category to prioritize sensitivity and avoid false negatives, despite these lesions generally being harmless. This limits direct comparison with other studies that classify vascular lesions as benign.
The model was trained on a subset of 9,947 images from HAM-10000 selected based on the availability of adequate segmentation masks, rather than the full dataset, which may introduce selection bias.

For the external ISIC 2018 test set, ground-truth masks were not available for all images; instead, masks predicted by the trained SegFormer-B5 model were used for preprocessing. This introduces a dependency on the segmentation model's accuracy for the cross-dataset evaluation.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance differences between models.

The best-performing configuration (SVM with Sigmoid kernel) achieved 93.17% accuracy and 84.22% F1-score on HAM-10000, but performance dropped to 87.7% accuracy and 82.01% macro F1-score on the external ISIC 2018 dataset, indicating a generalization gap.

Data augmentation strategies that preserved the original class distribution ('Augmented Original') outperformed those that balanced classes ('Augmented Equalized'), suggesting that maintaining natural prevalence is beneficial for this specific task.
